title: Chocolate, Cherry and Almond Fudge
subtitle: A thermometer pays for itself the first time.
excerpt: Chocolate, Cherry and Almond Fudge. A baking recipe from Rebecca's collection — straightforward, reliable, worth weighing rather than scooping.
sourceNotes: From Rebecca's personal cookbook — a recipe she's made many times in her own kitchen.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool fully, freeze in portions for up to three months. Defrost overnight in the fridge.

content.text: Chocolate, Cherry and Almond Fudge. A British home-cook's dish — the kind of recipe families adjust across generations. Adjustable for what you need.
Cookies bake fast and reward a chilled dough. Pull them out when the edges set and the middles still look slightly underdone.
Put the dates in a saucepan and add enough boiling water to cover them. Place the pan over a medium heat and simmer the dates gently for about 5 minutes or until they are really soft.
Meanwhile, toast the almonds, if using, for about 5 minutes in a dry frying pan over a low heat. Set them aside to cool slightly, then chop them roughly.

Tip the dates into a sieve to drain and discard the water. Transfer the dates to a food processor and leave them to cool for a few minutes. Add the nut butter, butter, maple syrup and cocoa powder and blend until smooth. Stir in the chopped nuts and dried cherries, if using.

Lightly grease a 25cm square shallow baking tray and line it with baking paper. Spoon the mixture into the tray and spread the surface flat with a spatula. Cover with another layer of baking paper and put the fudge in the freezer for 3 hours to set.

Remove the fudge from the freezer and let it defrost for 5 minutes before cutting it into small squares. Put the squares in a freezer-proof container and keep them in the freezer until needed. Allow the fudge to defrost for 5 minutes before you eat it.

You can leave out the almonds and cherries if you want something plainer or you can add other dried fruit or nuts, such as sultanas, dried apricots, hazelnuts, pecans and so on. Medjool dates do add a fab flavour but you can also use soft pitted dates which are cheaper. The fudge can be kept in the freezer for up to three months – if you’re really strong-willed! 137 calories per piece.

attrs.heading: If something goes wrong
attrs.intro: A few of the more common issues, and what tends to fix them.

items.cause: Sugar didn't reach the right stage, or temperature was guessed.
items.fix: Use a sugar thermometer. Stages: soft-ball 115°C, hard-ball 125°C, soft-crack 135°C, hard-crack 150°C. Cold-water tests work but the thermometer doesn't lie.

items.cause: Mixture stirred after it started bubbling, or a stray crystal landed on the side of the pan.
items.fix: Don't stir once the sugar is dissolved. Brush the inside of the pan with a wet pastry brush to wash crystals down.

items.cause: Beaten too soon — while still too hot.
items.fix: Cool to 43°C before beating. The mixture turns from glossy to matte when ready to set.
Add a teaspoon of warm cream or water and stir until smooth — this is the trick. Drier seizing needs more liquid; thin seizing wants less.

content.text: Flavoured version: stir in a teaspoon of vanilla, almond, or peppermint extract once the mixture is off the heat.
Dip-coated: once set, dip pieces in melted dark chocolate and let drip on baking paper.
Best fresh. Leftovers keep two days in the fridge; reheat covered with a splash of liquid.
A British home cook's dish. It sits among the recipes families remember without thinking about — the kind that gets adjusted across generations and never quite the same twice.
